author: inner_join
content:
If you already know JS array methods, you already know SQL.
SELECT is .map() (pick the shape you want), WHERE is .filter() (keep the rows you want), and ORDER BY is .sort().
The only real twist: SQL runs WHERE before SELECT, so you filter the rows first, then shape them. Same operations, just a different order.

comments.author: group_by
comments.content: The execution-order point is the part nobody tells beginners. WHERE can't see column aliases you create in SELECT because it ran first.

comments.author: window_fn
comments.content: Add GROUP BY = .reduce() and the whole mental map clicks into place.

author: group_by
content:
Classic interview question: "What's the difference between WHERE and HAVING?"
WHERE filters rows before they're grouped. HAVING filters groups after aggregation.
Rule of thumb: if your condition uses COUNT, SUM, or AVG, it belongs in HAVING. Anything on raw columns goes in WHERE.
code:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS orders
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'paid'      -- filter rows first
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;       -- then filter groups

comments.author: null_handler
comments.content: Putting both in is the power move: WHERE narrows the dataset cheaply, then HAVING handles the aggregate. Faster than dumping everything into HAVING.

author: null_handler
content:
Spot the bug. This query is supposed to find users who never placed an order. It returns zero rows every time.
Why? NULL is not a value, it's the absence of one. Comparing anything to NULL with = yields NULL, never true.
Use IS NULL, never = NULL.

comments.author: bobby_tables
comments.content: This bites people in JS too where == null is truthy for both null and undefined. SQL is stricter: NULL infects every comparison it touches.

comments.author: inner_join
comments.content: Same trap in JOINs and NOT IN. If your subquery returns a NULL, NOT IN silently returns nothing. Use NOT EXISTS instead.

author: window_fn
content:
Stop self-joining a table just to compare a row to the previous one. Window functions do it cleanly.
LAG() grabs the value from the prior row, OVER defines the ordering. It's like array[i] vs array[i-1], but declarative.
Great for month-over-month growth, running totals, and "days since last login."
code:
SELECT
  month,
  revenue,
  revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS change
FROM monthly_sales;

comments.author: group_by
comments.content: PARTITION BY is the unlock. Add PARTITION BY product_id and you get per-product running comparisons without a single join.

author: inner_join
content:
JOINs explained like you're matching socks.
INNER JOIN: only keep socks that have a pair. Lonely socks get tossed.
LEFT JOIN: keep every sock from the left drawer, pair it if you can, otherwise leave the partner slot empty (NULL).
It's basically a lookup: for each row on the left, go find the matching row on the right.
code:
SELECT o.id, c.name
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN customers c
  ON o.customer_id = c.id;
-- orders with no customer still show, name = NULL

comments.author: null_handler
comments.content: And that NULL from a LEFT JOIN is your debugging gold. Filter WHERE c.id IS NULL to find orphaned orders with no matching customer.

author: bobby_tables
content:
Hot take: SELECT * in production code is a bug waiting to happen.
It breaks the moment someone adds a column, ships extra bytes over the wire, and makes your query's intent invisible in code review.
Name your columns. Future you, reading the query at 2am, will know exactly what data the app actually depends on.

comments.author: window_fn
comments.content: SELECT * in an exploratory query in your SQL editor? Fine. In an app that other code parses by column position? Genuinely dangerous.

comments.author: inner_join
comments.content: It also nukes covering-index optimizations. The planner can serve named columns from the index alone, but * forces a trip to the table.

author: group_by
content:
TIL: COUNT(*) and COUNT(column) are not the same thing.
COUNT(*) counts every row. COUNT(email) counts only rows where email is not NULL.
So COUNT(*) - COUNT(email) tells you exactly how many users are missing an email. A one-line data-quality check.
code:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total,
  COUNT(email) AS with_email,
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(email) AS missing_email
FROM users;

comments.author: null_handler
comments.content: And COUNT(DISTINCT email) gives unique non-null emails. Three flavors of COUNT, three different questions answered.

author: null_handler
content:
If you've used Pandas, GROUP BY is just df.groupby().agg().
SELECT region, SUM(sales) FROM t GROUP BY region is df.groupby('region')['sales'].sum().
The mental model is the same in both: split the data into buckets by a key, then collapse each bucket into one summary row.
code:
-- SQL
SELECT region, SUM(sales)
FROM orders
GROUP BY region;

# Pandas
df.groupby('region')['sales'].sum()

comments.author: bobby_tables
comments.content: The gotcha coming from Pandas: in SQL every non-aggregated column in SELECT must appear in GROUP BY. Pandas is more forgiving, SQL makes you be explicit.

author: bobby_tables
content:
Never build SQL with string concatenation from user input. That's literally how my namesake works.
Use parameterized queries. The driver sends your query and the values separately, so input can never be parsed as SQL.
It's not just security theater, it also handles quoting and escaping for you correctly every time.

comments.author: group_by
comments.content: Little Bobby Tables, we call him. The xkcd is funny until it's your prod users table getting dropped.

comments.author: window_fn
comments.content: Parameterized queries also let the DB cache the query plan since the SQL text is stable across calls. Security and speed in one.

author: window_fn
content:
Why is my query slow? A starter checklist before you blame the database.
1. Is there an index on the column in your WHERE clause? No index = full table scan.
2. Are you wrapping the indexed column in a function? WHERE LOWER(email) = ... ignores the index on email.
3. Run EXPLAIN. It shows whether the planner uses your index or scans every row, like console.log for query performance.
code:
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 42;
-- Look for 'Index Scan' good, 'Seq Scan' on big tables bad

comments.author: inner_join
comments.content: Point 2 is sneaky. WHERE created_at::date = '2024-01-01' kills the index. Rewrite as a range: created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2024-01-02'.

comments.author: null_handler
comments.content: Also check selectivity. An index on a boolean that's true for 90% of rows is nearly useless, the planner will scan anyway.
